forecasts.reasoning:
The resolution criteria require a law that "fully prohibits" the sale of THC in Texas. Senate Bill 3 (SB 3) was introduced this session as a priority for Lt. Gov. Dan Patrick to completely ban the sale of hemp-derived THC products. The Senate passed this total ban in March 2025. However, the Texas House has historically favored regulation and business interests over total bans. In early May 2025, a House committee unanimously passed a substitute version of SB 3 that transforms the bill from a total ban into a strict regulatory framework. Crucially, the House substitute explicitly allows the continued sale of beverages containing up to 10 mg of THC and certain low-THC consumables. 

Under the question's criteria, a law that allows some THC sales (like the House's 10mg drinks) would be considered a "partial ban" and resolve as NO. With the regular legislative session ending on June 2, 2025 (Sine Die), the two chambers would need to reconcile these vastly different approaches if the House passes its version. Given the unanimous committee support in the House for regulation over a ban, and the strong lobbying presence of the Texas hemp industry, it is highly unlikely the House will completely capitulate to the Senate's total ban. A compromise involving potency caps or a complete failure to pass the bill before the session ends are both far more likely outcomes, both of which would resolve to NO.

Mahmoud Khalil, a lawful permanent resident, was ruled "removable" by an immigration judge on April 11, 2025. However, this ruling only initiated the next phase of his legal defense. His attorneys had until April 23 to file applications for relief from deportation, including asylum, which requires further immigration court hearings. If denied, he has the right to appeal to the Board of Immigration Appeals (BIA), which typically takes months to years. In parallel, a federal district judge in New Jersey (Judge Michael Farbiarz) is overseeing Khalil’s habeas corpus lawsuit and has expressly prohibited the government from deporting him while those proceedings are active. On April 29, Judge Farbiarz ruled that Khalil's federal lawsuit can proceed, and on May 7, he demanded the government detail its legal grounds for his deportation. Given the multiple layers of pending appeals, mandatory hearings for his relief claims, and an active federal stay on his deportation, it is highly improbable that the legal process will be exhausted and he will be physically deported before July 1, 2025 (less than two months away from the May 14 cutoff). Legal experts estimate the process will take 1-2 years even if he remains detained.

The resolution date (July 1, 2025) is only 1.5 months away from the knowledge cutoff (mid-May 2025). While internal EPA communications indicate plans to "sunset" and eliminate the Energy Star program under the Trump administration, shutting down a 30-year-old federal program with over $100 billion in annual related commerce, international ties (Canada uses EPA's API for its own certifications), and embedded tax-credit infrastructure takes considerable time. Furthermore, Energy Star was codified into law via the Energy Policy Act of 2005, which could pose legal barriers or slow down the dismantling process. Even if the program is completely defunded, federal websites typically do not go completely offline (yielding a domain resolution or host connection failure) in a matter of weeks; at a minimum, the domain usually remains active to host an archive or an informational shutdown message. Therefore, it is highly probable that IsItDownRightNow will still read the domain as "UP" on July 1, 2025.

Circle made its S-1 public on April 1, 2025, intending to list under the ticker 'CRCL' on the NYSE by May or June. However, on April 4–6, widespread reports indicated that Circle, along with other high-profile companies like Klarna and StubHub, shelved its investor roadshow and delayed the IPO due to market turbulence and general uncertainty surrounding new US trade tariffs. With no news of the roadshow resuming as of mid-May and the 135-day stale financial rule likely forcing Circle to update its prospectus with Q1 2025 financials before proceeding, the timeline to successfully relaunch, conduct a roadshow, and officially list before the July 1 deadline is very tight. While a late June debut remains possible if market conditions dramatically improve, the delay makes a slip into Q3 much more probable.

The only scheduled FOMC meeting before the July 1, 2025 deadline is on June 18, 2025. Over the course of early 2025, the Federal Reserve has held the federal funds rate steady at 4.25%-4.5%, pointing to a resilient economy. Rate cut expectations for the June meeting have steadily declined. Initially, markets priced in a high probability (~78% in late April) for a June rate cut. However, following likely strong economic data in early May and a hawkish tone from Fed Chair Jerome Powell at the May 7 FOMC meeting ("We don't have to be in a hurry. The economy is resilient"), markets sharply recalibrated. By mid-May, sources report that fixed income markets view a June cut as unlikely, pricing the subsequent July 30 meeting as only a 50/50 chance. Market-implied odds for a June cut fell below 40% by early May and were likely pressured even lower by the May 7 FOMC outcomes. Unless there is a sudden, severe macroeconomic shock or unexpectedly weak jobs/inflation data released before mid-June, the Fed is expected to use the June meeting to update projections rather than enact a cut. The probability of an off-cycle cut or a surprise June cut is estimated at roughly 18%.

The objective to "terminate the Public Service Loan Forgiveness program" is unlikely to be marked as "complete" on the Project 2025 Tracker before July 1, 2025. Given that PSLF was established by Congress (in 2007), it cannot be completely terminated by executive action alone. While the Trump administration has issued executive orders limiting the program (reclassifying "public service" to exclude certain nonprofits) and initiated a rulemaking process, these actions only restrict the program rather than terminate it, justifying its current "in progress" status. 

To formally terminate PSLF, Congress would need to pass legislation, most likely as part of the upcoming budget reconciliation package. Although Congress passed the budget resolution in April 2025 to unlock reconciliation, drafting, CBO-scoring, negotiating, and passing a massive reconciliation bill (expected to cover expansive tax cuts and spending cuts) typically takes many months. Achieving this within the short remaining window (between mid-May and July 1, 2025) is highly improbable, as such bills usually face delays over intra-party negotiations and Senate procedural hurdles (the Byrd Rule) stretching into the fall or winter. Even if the bill were passed by the deadline, it is not certain that Republicans would fully terminate PSLF instead of heavily reforming or grandfathering it. Thus, the spreadsheet will overwhelmingly likely still categorize the objective as either "in progress" or similar by the resolution date.
